subject: EU Law
lesson_title: Indirect Effect and Limits in Criminal Proceedings

questions.question: The doctrine of indirect effect (also called the 'interpretive obligation') was established by the Court of Justice in which case?

options.A: Marshall v Southampton and South West Hampshire Area Health Authority [1986]
options.B: Von Colson and Kamann v Land Nordrhein-Westfalen [1984]
options.C: Marleasing SA v La Comercial Internacional de Alimentación SA [1990]

questions.explanation: The doctrine of indirect effect was established in Von Colson and Kamann v Land Nordrhein-Westfalen [1984] CJEU. Two social workers were refused employment by a prison (a public body) and argued that the Equal Treatment Directive required adequate remedies for discrimination. Rather than holding the directive directly effective on the level of compensation, the Court held that national courts are required under what is now Article 4(3) TEU to interpret national law in light of the wording and purpose of a directive in order to achieve the result required by the directive.

questions.question: What is the significance of Marleasing SA v La Comercial Internacional de Alimentación SA [1990] CJEU?

options.A: It established that directives can have direct horizontal effect between private parties
options.B: It confirmed and extended indirect effect to horizontal disputes between private parties, and formulated the 'as far as possible' interpretive obligation
options.C: It held that unimplemented directives cannot be relied upon in national courts in any circumstances
options.D: It established that national courts must disapply national law that conflicts with an unimplemented directive

questions.explanation: Marleasing SA v La Comercial Internacional de Alimentación SA [1990] CJEU is the defining case on indirect effect. Its significance is twofold: first, the case involved a dispute between two private Spanish companies — confirming that indirect effect operates in horizontal disputes between private parties, filling the gap left by the absence of horizontal direct effect of directives. Second, the Court held that national courts must interpret national law 'as far as possible' in light of the wording and purpose of the directive to achieve the result pursued by it. This formulation is central to the scope and limits of indirect effect.

questions.question: The phrase 'as far as possible' in the Marleasing formulation of indirect effect is critical because it:

options.A: Means national courts must always achieve the result of the directive regardless of the wording of national law
options.B: Signals that indirect effect is an interpretive obligation — the national court uses the directive as an interpretive guide but cannot interpret national law contra legem
options.C: Allows the Court of Justice to substitute its own interpretation for that of the national court
options.D: Gives member states a discretion to limit the scope of indirect effect in their national legal systems

questions.explanation: The phrase 'as far as possible' is fundamental to the scope of indirect effect. Indirect effect is a doctrine of interpretation, not direct replacement of national law. The national court uses the directive as an interpretive guide when applying national law. There is a limit: the national court cannot achieve a result through interpretation that is impossible given the wording of national law — it cannot interpret national law contra legem, meaning in a way that is directly contrary to its clear terms. This limit was illustrated in Duke v GEC Reliance [1988] where the House of Lords held it could not distort the Sex Discrimination Act 1975 to give effect to a directive.

questions.question: The indirect effect obligation applies to which national legislation?

options.A: Only to legislation enacted specifically to implement the directive in question
options.B: Only to legislation enacted after the directive came into force
options.C: To all national legislation — whether enacted before or after the directive — as confirmed in Marleasing
options.D: Only to national legislation in the precise subject matter area covered by the directive

questions.explanation: The indirect effect obligation applies to national legislation enacted both before and after the directive, as confirmed in Marleasing [1990]. This is important because it means that pre-existing national law must also be interpreted in conformity with a directive where possible. In Pfeiffer and others v Deutsches Rotes Kreuz [2004] CJEU, the Court further emphasised that the national court must consider national law as a whole — not just the specific provision at issue — to assess whether it can be interpreted in conformity with the directive.

questions.question: In Kolpinghuis Nijmegen [1987] CJEU, the Court of Justice established a critically important limit on indirect effect. What was this limit?

options.A: Indirect effect cannot be used to extend the scope of a regulation beyond its express terms
options.B: Indirect effect cannot be used to determine or aggravate criminal liability on the basis of an unimplemented directive
options.C: Indirect effect does not apply to directives that have expired or been repealed
options.D: Indirect effect cannot be used in proceedings where the state is a party

questions.explanation: In Kolpinghuis Nijmegen [1987] CJEU, the Court held that the obligation to interpret national law in conformity with a directive cannot have the effect of determining or aggravating, on the basis of the directive and in the absence of implementing national legislation, the liability in criminal law of persons. A bar owner was prosecuted for selling carbonated water that did not meet the mineral water standard in an unimplemented directive. The Dutch prosecution sought to rely on the directive indirectly to support the criminal charge. The Court refused: criminal liability must be based on national law, and individuals must be able to know from national law what conduct is prohibited.

questions.question: What was the issue in the Kolpinghuis Nijmegen case, and why did the Court refuse to permit indirect effect?

options.A: A Dutch bar owner was sued in civil proceedings by a competitor — the Court refused indirect effect because the directive had not yet entered into force
options.B: A Dutch bar owner faced criminal prosecution for selling water that did not meet the mineral water standard in an unimplemented directive — the Court refused because using indirect effect to create criminal liability would violate legal certainty and non-retroactivity
options.C: An Irish company was prosecuted for tax evasion under rules derived from an unimplemented directive — the Court refused because tax law is reserved to member states
options.D: A German employer was prosecuted for breaching health and safety standards in an unimplemented directive — the Court refused because employment law falls outside EU competence

questions.explanation: In Kolpinghuis Nijmegen [1987] CJEU, a bar owner in the Netherlands was prosecuted for selling carbonated water that, according to the standard in an unimplemented EU directive, did not meet the definition of mineral water. The Dutch prosecution sought to rely on the directive indirectly through national law to support the criminal charge. The Court held this was impermissible because using an unimplemented directive indirectly to create or extend criminal liability violates the fundamental principles of legal certainty and non-retroactivity — an individual must be able to know, from national law, what conduct is prohibited and what penalties attach.

questions.question: Arcaro [1996] CJEU confirmed and extended the Kolpinghuis principle. What additional point did Arcaro establish?

options.A: That indirect effect can never be used in environmental law cases regardless of criminal liability
options.B: That the interpretive obligation cannot serve as the basis for an interpretation that would oblige a national court to go against provisions of national law that run counter to the directive
options.C: That indirect effect applies only to directives adopted by the ordinary legislative procedure
options.D: That the criminal liability limit applies only to natural persons, not to corporations

questions.explanation: In Arcaro [1996] CJEU, an Italian factory owner was prosecuted for exceeding industrial discharge limits set by an EU directive that had not been fully implemented in Italy. The Court confirmed the Kolpinghuis principle and extended it: the interpretive obligation not only cannot be used to create or aggravate criminal liability, but further cannot serve as the basis for an interpretation that would oblige a national court to go against provisions of national law that run counter to the directive. The combination of the contra legem limit and the criminal liability limit defines the outer boundaries of indirect effect.

questions.question: The rationale behind the rule that indirect effect cannot be used to determine or aggravate criminal liability is based on which fundamental legal principles?

options.A: The principles of subsidiarity and proportionality
options.B: The principles of legal certainty and non-retroactivity — individuals must be able to know from national law what conduct is prohibited
options.C: The principle of direct effect — unimplemented directives cannot have any effect in national law
options.D: The principle of institutional balance — criminal law is a matter for the member states alone

questions.explanation: The rule that indirect effect cannot be used to determine or aggravate criminal liability is rooted in the fundamental principles of legal certainty and non-retroactivity. Criminal liability must be based on national law. An individual must be able to know, from national law, what conduct is prohibited and what criminal consequences flow from it. To use an unimplemented EU directive indirectly — through the interpretive obligation — to create, extend, or aggravate criminal liability would undermine these fundamental guarantees, because the national law that the individual could read would not on its face impose the criminal liability being imposed.

questions.question: In Von Colson [1984], the Court chose indirect effect rather than direct effect to address the inadequate remedy. Why was this significant for horizontal disputes?

options.A: It meant that all directives automatically have direct horizontal effect in private law disputes
options.B: It provided a mechanism — the interpretive obligation — through which directives could influence private law disputes even though directives lack horizontal direct effect
options.C: It removed the distinction between public and private bodies for the purposes of directive enforcement
options.D: It allowed private individuals to sue member states directly for failure to implement directives

questions.explanation: The significance of indirect effect in relation to horizontal disputes is that it provides a mechanism through which directives can reach private law relationships even without horizontal direct effect. As established in Marshall v Southampton Area Health Authority [1986] CJEU, directives do not have direct horizontal effect — they cannot be relied upon directly against a private party. But through Marleasing [1990], national courts must interpret national law (which does bind private parties) 'as far as possible' in light of the directive. Indirect effect thus partially fills the horizontal gap, though it is limited by the contra legem rule.

questions.question: In Duke v GEC Reliance [1988] (UK), the House of Lords declined to apply indirect effect to interpret the Sex Discrimination Act 1975 in conformity with the Equal Treatment Directive. On what basis?

options.A: The Equal Treatment Directive had direct horizontal effect and the House of Lords was bound to apply it directly
options.B: Lord Templeman held that it would be straining rather than construing the language of the Act — the plain meaning of the national law could not be distorted to achieve the directive's result
options.C: The Sex Discrimination Act 1975 had been enacted after the directive and therefore could not benefit from the interpretive obligation
options.D: The House of Lords held that indirect effect does not apply to pre-Marleasing national legislation

questions.explanation: In Duke v GEC Reliance [1988], the House of Lords held that the Sex Discrimination Act 1975 — which predated the Equal Treatment Directive — could not be interpreted to give effect to the directive's requirements. Lord Templeman stated famously that it would be 'straining rather than construing' the language of the Act to achieve the directive's result. This illustrates the contra legem limit on indirect effect: national courts cannot distort the plain meaning of national law. The interpretive obligation operates 'as far as possible' — but not beyond what the national law can bear linguistically and contextually.
